title: Iran Doesn't Need a Single Missile to Collapse the American Empire | Prof. Jiang Xueqin
full_text: Let me tell you something that nobody ever taught you in school. Something that explains more about the modern world than almost anything else you could possibly learn. And I promise you, once you understand this, you're going to start seeing global news, global conflicts, and global economics in a completely different way. Because there is one single mechanism that holds American power together. One foundation underneath everything. The military, the economy, the political influence, all of it rests on this one thing. And that thing is called the petro dollar system. Most people have never heard of it. And that's not an accident. See, when people try to explain why America is so powerful, they usually talk about the military. They talk about technology. They talk about the size of the economy. And those things are real, but they're not the root cause. They're the result. The actual source of American dominance is something much more specific, much more deliberate, and much more fragile than most people realize. It comes down to oil. And more specifically, it comes down to the currency the world uses to buy oil. To understand how we got here, you have to go back to the early 1970s. Before that, the global financial system worked under something called the Breton Woods Agreement. Under that framework, the US dollar had a very specific guarantee attached to it. Every dollar in circulation was backed by gold. Any country holding dollars could exchange them for actual physical gold stored in American vaults. That's what gave the dollar its credibility. That's why the world trusted it. It wasn't just paper. It was a claim on something real. But then America got into serious financial trouble. The Vietnam War was extraordinarily expensive. The government was spending money it didn't have, printing dollars to cover the gap. And slowly other countries started doing the math. They started realizing there were far more dollars floating around the world than there was gold to back them up. France famously started sending ships across the Atlantic to exchange dollars for gold before the whole thing unraveled. The game was up and in 1971, President Nixon made it official. He closed what was called the gold window. The dollar was no longer convertible to gold. Just like that, the anchor was gone. Now, think about what that moment actually meant. The dollar was now just paper. It was backed by nothing except faith in the American government. Why would the rest of the world continue to hold dollars? Why would countries price their goods in dollars? Why would international trade continue to be conducted in a currency that no longer had any physical backing? America had a serious problem, a potentially catastrophic problem. Because if global demand for dollars collapsed, the American economy would collapse along with it. So Washington went looking for a solution and they found one in the most important commodity on the planet. The United States approached Saudi Arabia and they made a deal that would quietly reshape the entire world order. The terms were straightforward. Saudi Arabia would agree to sell its oil exclusively in US dollars. Every barrel to every buyer anywhere in the world will be priced and paid for in dollars. Not pounds, not Deutsch marks, not any other currency, dollars only. And in exchange, America would guarantee the survival of the Saudi regime. Military protection, weapons sales, political cover, whatever the royal family needed to stay in power, America would provide. The Saudis agreed. Other OPEC nations followed, and the petro dollar system was born. Now, here is why this deal was so extraordinarily powerful. Think about what oil means to the world. Every modern economy runs on energy. Every factory, every car, every power grid, every shipping vessel. Without oil, the entire industrial world stops functioning. And in the 1970s, Saudi Arabia and its Gulf neighbors controlled an enormous share of global oil production. So when they said oil would only be sold in dollars, they were essentially saying something to every country on Earth. If you want your economy to function, you need dollars first. Japan has to get dollars before it can buy energy. Germany has to get dollars. India has to get dollars. South Korea, Brazil, Indonesia, every country that imports oil has to first acquire US dollars. That creates a permanent structural global demand for the American currency. Not because people particularly love the dollar, not because the American economy is so impressive, but because the most essential commodity in the world can only be purchased with one specific currency. And there's a second layer to this that makes it even more powerful. When Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states collect all these dollars from selling oil, what do they do with them? They can't just leave them sitting there. They reinvest them. They take those dollars and pour them back into American financial markets, treasury bonds, which means they're lending money directly to the US government, American stocks, American real estate, American financial institutions. This recycling of oil money back into the American economy is called petro dollar recycling and it is one of the most important and least discussed financial mechanisms in the world. Because what it means is that America gets to run a kind of economic model that no other country on earth can replicate. The government can spend more than it earns year after year because there is always a ready market for its debt. The financial markets can grow and grow because foreign capital is constantly flowing in. Asset prices stay elevated, borrowing costs stay low, and the whole machine keeps running. Not because of extraordinary productivity or innovation alone, but because of this structural arrangement that forces capital into American markets from around the world. This is why the American economy looks the way it does. It's not primarily a manufacturing economy anymore. It's a financial economy. The real wealth is generated on Wall Street, in the stock market, in the bond market, in real estate. And all of that depends on one thing continuing to function. The world must keep needing dollars to buy oil. But the dollar is more than just a currency. This is the part that starts to explain a lot of modern geopolitics. Because the dollar is also the most powerful financial weapon ever created. Since the entire global financial system is built on dollar infrastructure, the United States has the ability to exclude any country from that system. They can freeze assets. They can block international payments. They can make it impossible for a country to conduct normal trade with the rest of the world. We watched this happen in real time with Russia after 2022. We've watched it with Iran for decades with Venezuela, with Cuba. When Washington decides to punish a government, it reaches for the dollar as its first weapon. And that weapon is devastating. But here is the unintended consequence of using that weapon so aggressively. Every time America cuts a country off from the dollar system, every other government in the world watches and learns a lesson. And the lesson they learn is that dependence on the dollar is a vulnerability that any country at any time could find itself on the wrong side of Washington and suddenly unable to function in the global economy. So they start asking a very rational question. How do we reduce our exposure to this risk? And that question is driving the most significant shift in the global financial system in 50 years. China has been the most deliberate and most serious about pursuing an alternative. Beijing is the largest oil importer on the planet. They buy enormous quantities from the Middle East, from Russia, from Africa. And they have been systematically working to convince oil producing nations to accept Chinese UN instead of dollars. They created UN denominated oil futures contracts. They've negotiated bilateral currency arrangements with trading partners across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. Russia after being hit with sweeping sanctions moved quickly to price its energy exports in rubles and yen. Iran has been trading outside the dollar for years and smaller countries that feel vulnerable to American pressure are quietly building alternative payment infrastructure. Meanwhile, the relationship between China and the Gulf States has undergone a dramatic transformation. Chinese investment is flowing into Saudi Arabia, into the UAE, across the entire region. Trade ties have deepened enormously. Technology partnerships, infrastructure projects, energy agreements, the economic center of gravity in the Middle East is gradually shifting eastward. And Saudi Arabia, which for half a century has been the cornerstone of the petrod dollar system, is now openly diversifying its relationships in ways that would have been unthinkable 20 years ago. This is the real story underneath so much of what you see in the news. The tensions, the realignments, the competition for influence across Africa, the Middle East, Central Asia, underneath all of it is a struggle over the future of the global currency system. And the stakes couldn't be higher because here is what actually happens. If the petrod dollar system breaks down, if oil exporting nations begin accepting yuan, euros, gold, or other currencies at significant scale, the global demand for dollars starts to shrink. Countries no longer need to maintain massive dollar reserves. They don't need to keep buying American Treasury bonds to keep their dollar holdings productive. and Gulf sovereign wealth funds instead of pouring capital into American financial markets start directing it toward Chinese markets toward Asian infrastructure toward alternatives. The consequences for America are severe. The government needs to borrow constantly to cover its annual deficits. That balling depends entirely on there being strong global demand for US debt. If that demand weakens, interest rates rise. And rising interest rates destabilize almost everything in the American economic model. Companies that expanded on cheap debt face crushing repayment costs. Asset valuations that were built on the assumption of cheap money forever start to look completely unrealistic. The stock market, which has become the primary measure of American economic health and the primary source of retirement wealth for ordinary Americans, becomes deeply vulnerable. And then confidence becomes the real issue because financial systems at their deepest level run on belief. The dollar is powerful because people believe it's powerful. American financial markets attract capital because people believe they're the safest place to put money. The moment that belief starts to seriously erode, the dynamics can shift faster than anyone is prepared for. Confidence doesn't leak out slowly and then collapse slowly. It leaks slowly and then evaporates overnight. Now, I want to be honest with you. This is not something that's going to fully play out in a year or two. The dollar is deeply embedded in global trade, in financial contracts, in banking infrastructure built over decades. There is no ready replacement that the world can seamlessly switch to tomorrow. The yuan has real limitations. Chinese capital markets are not fully open. Europe is too fragmented. These transitions are slow and complicated. But slow is not the same as not happening. And the direction is unmistakable. More energy deals outside the dollar every year. More countries building payment systems that bypass American financial infrastructure. More governments quietly reducing their dollar reserves. More sovereign wealth funds diversifying away from American assets. The foundation is not collapsing, but it is being steadily, deliberately, and systematically undermined. Every major geopolitical story you follow connects back to this. Why does America maintain such an enormous military presence in the Middle East? Why does Washington react so aggressively to any country that tries to price oil in another currency? Why is the competition between the US and China so intense and so broad, covering everything from semiconductors to undersea cables to African infrastructure loans? Because underneath all of it is one question. One question that determines who holds real power in the 21st century. And that question is simply this. What currency does the world use to buy energy? Whoever controls the answer to that question controls the global economy. They control who can be punished and who can be protected. They control which governments survive and which ones struggle. They control the flow of capital, the cost of borrowing, the fate of financial markets across the planet. America has controlled that answer for 50 years. And right now, for the first time in half a century, that control is genuinely being challenged.
